title: Three Gorges Dam — the biggest power station on Earth
what: China's Three Gorges Dam on the Yangtze has a capacity of about 22,500 megawatts, more than any other power plant.
why: Its 32 huge turbines generate enormous amounts of electricity and help control deadly downstream floods.
care: One structure produces roughly as much power as 20 large nuclear reactors — a stark display of hydropower's scale and its human cost.

title: Baihetan — a dam with record-breaking turbines
what: China's Baihetan Dam, around 16,000 megawatts, uses the world's first 1,000-megawatt hydro turbines.
why: Each single turbine there can power a small city, pushing hydro engineering to new limits.
care: It shows how clean hydropower keeps scaling up, even as good dam sites grow scarce worldwide.

title: Bath County — the world's largest 'battery'
what: Virginia's Bath County Pumped Storage Station stores energy by pumping water between two reservoirs, with about 3,000 megawatts of capacity.
why: Often called the world's largest battery, it has banked surplus power since 1985 — long before lithium.
care: It's living proof that grids have stored electricity at massive scale for decades using just water and gravity.

title: Dinorwig — the power station inside a mountain
what: Wales' Dinorwig pumped-storage plant is buried inside a slate mountain and can hit full power of about 1,800 megawatts in well under 20 seconds.
why: Its astonishing speed lets it catch sudden surges — famously when millions of kettles switch on after a big TV broadcast.
care: It's a hidden engineering marvel quietly smoothing out a whole nation's electricity demand.

title: Kashiwazaki-Kariwa — the largest nuclear plant
what: Japan's Kashiwazaki-Kariwa station has the greatest generating capacity of any nuclear plant, with seven reactors totalling around 7,900 megawatts.
why: It shows how much carbon-free power a single nuclear site can hold, though it has spent years offline for safety upgrades.
care: Its on-and-off status captures Japan's whole post-Fukushima struggle over the future of nuclear power.

title: Bruce — North America's nuclear giant
what: The Bruce Nuclear Generating Station in Ontario, Canada, is among the largest operating nuclear plants in the world by output.
why: Its eight reactors supply a large share of Ontario's electricity with almost no carbon emissions.
care: It helped make Ontario's grid one of the cleanest in North America after the province phased out coal.

title: North Sea Link — a cable between two countries' grids
what: The North Sea Link is a roughly 720-kilometre subsea cable connecting the power grids of the UK and Norway.
why: Britain can import Norwegian hydropower when needed and export surplus wind power back — a two-way clean-energy trade.
care: Such interconnectors let countries share renewables, smoothing out each other's calm or cloudy days.

title: A buried line bringing hydro to New York City
what: The Champlain Hudson Power Express is a high-voltage line buried underground and underwater to carry Canadian hydropower into New York City.
why: Burying it avoids the visual and permitting battles that block many overhead transmission projects.
care: It's a model for delivering distant clean power into dense cities without a forest of new pylons.

title: Europe's vast synchronous grid
what: Most of continental Europe runs as a single synchronised grid, stretching from Spain to Turkey and Poland, all beating at 50 hertz together.
why: It's among the largest synchronous machines ever built, letting dozens of countries trade power instantly.
care: It means a windy day in Germany can help keep the lights on in France — cross-border cooperation as physics.

title: North America's giant interconnections
what: The U.S. and Canada run mainly on a few enormous synchronised grids, the largest being the Eastern Interconnection covering much of the continent.
why: Thousands of generators across many states and provinces all stay perfectly in step within each interconnection.
care: It's one of the most complex machines humans have built — and it has to work flawlessly every second.

title: Texas — the grid that stands alone
what: Texas runs its own grid (ERCOT) largely disconnected from the rest of the U.S., partly to avoid federal regulation.
why: Independence gives it freedom but also fewer neighbours to borrow power from in a crisis, as a deadly 2021 freeze showed.
care: It's a real-world lesson in the trade-off between grid independence and the safety net of interconnection.

title: The Snowy Mountains Scheme
what: Australia's Snowy Mountains Scheme, built over 25 years, threads 16 dams and seven power stations through tunnels in the mountains.
why: It generates hydropower while diverting water inland for irrigation — a postwar feat of engineering and migration-driven labour.
care: Its modern 'Snowy 2.0' expansion aims to become a giant battery for Australia's growing solar and wind.

title: Quebec's James Bay hydro complex
what: Quebec's James Bay project dammed vast northern rivers, with the Robert-Bourassa station alone exceeding 5,000 megawatts.
why: It made Quebec a hydropower powerhouse that exports clean electricity to neighbouring provinces and U.S. states.
care: It also reshaped Indigenous lands and lives — a reminder that even clean power has real human costs.

title: Itaipu — a dam shared by two nations
what: The Itaipu Dam on the Paraná River is jointly owned and operated by Brazil and Paraguay, with about 14,000 megawatts of capacity.
why: It's a rare bi-national power plant, splitting both the electricity and the governance between two countries.
care: It supplies most of Paraguay's power and a big share of Brazil's — proof that shared energy projects can work.

title: Kariba — the world's largest reservoir
what: The Kariba Dam between Zambia and Zimbabwe created the largest artificial reservoir by volume on Earth.
why: It supplies much of both countries' electricity, but droughts can drop the water so low that power output collapses.
care: It shows how climate and rainfall can directly decide whether the lights stay on in hydro-dependent nations.

title: The Aswan High Dam
what: Completed in 1970, Egypt's Aswan High Dam tamed the Nile's annual floods and generated hydropower for the nation.
why: It transformed Egypt's agriculture and electricity supply, but trapped fertile silt and displaced communities.
care: It's a textbook case of a megaproject's sweeping benefits and lasting side-effects.

title: Noor Ouarzazate — a solar oasis
what: Morocco's Noor Ouarzazate complex is one of the world's largest concentrated solar power plants, using mirrors to focus sunlight and store heat.
why: By storing heat in molten salt, it can keep generating for hours after sunset — solar that runs into the night.
care: It points to how sun-rich nations could export clean power and reduce reliance on imported fuel.

title: Gansu — a wind farm the size of a county
what: China's Gansu (Jiuquan) Wind Power Base in the Gobi Desert is one of the largest wind projects ever attempted, planned in the tens of gigawatts.
why: Concentrating thousands of turbines in one windy desert pushes wind power to industrial scale.
care: Its early struggle to send power to distant cities drove China's huge build-out of ultra-long transmission lines.

title: Olkiluoto 3 — Europe's nuclear comeback
what: Finland's Olkiluoto 3 reactor, around 1,600 megawatts, finally began regular output in 2023 after years of delays.
why: It's one of Europe's most powerful single reactors and noticeably lowered Finnish electricity prices once running.
care: Its troubled build, then steady payoff, captures both the promise and the pain of modern nuclear projects.

title: Grand Inga — the dam that could power a continent
what: On the Congo River, the existing Inga dams could be expanded into 'Grand Inga', a proposed complex larger than any power plant on Earth.
why: The river's immense, steady flow could in theory supply a huge share of Africa's electricity.
care: Decades of stalled plans show how finance, politics, and governance — not just engineering — decide if mega-power gets built.

title: Hoover Dam — a Depression-era icon
what: The 221-metre Hoover Dam on the Colorado River supplies power and water across the U.S. Southwest and was a marvel of 1930s engineering.
why: It demonstrated that public works on a grand scale could reshape a region's economy and landscape.
care: Falling water levels behind it today are a visible warning of drought's threat to hydropower.

title: Grand Coulee — America's biggest power producer
what: The Grand Coulee Dam on the Columbia River is the largest electricity producer in the United States.
why: Its cheap, plentiful power fuelled the Pacific Northwest's industry and aluminium smelting.
care: It shows how a single piece of infrastructure can decide where industries and cities grow.

title: Moss Landing — a battery on a power-plant site
what: California's Moss Landing, on the grounds of a former gas plant, is one of the world's largest battery storage facilities.
why: It stores cheap midday solar and discharges it into the evening peak, replacing fossil 'peaker' plants.
care: It's a glimpse of how old fossil sites can be repurposed into the storage hubs of a cleaner grid.

title: Iceland — a grid run on heat and water
what: Iceland generates essentially all its electricity from hydropower and geothermal energy tapped from its volcanic landscape.
why: Cheap, clean, abundant power lets it run energy-hungry industries like aluminium smelting and data centres.
care: It's a real example of a modern economy powered almost entirely by renewables — thanks to unique geology.

title: Cables under the English Channel
what: High-voltage links run beneath the English Channel, letting Britain and France trade electricity directly.
why: When one country has surplus nuclear or wind power, the other can buy it within seconds.
care: These quiet undersea cables turn neighbouring nations into a shared, more resilient power market.

title: Tehri — India's towering dam
what: The Tehri Dam in the Himalayan foothills is one of the tallest dams in the world and a major source of Indian hydropower and irrigation.
why: It combines electricity, water storage, and pumped-storage capability in a seismically active region.
care: It highlights the engineering challenge of building huge dams safely in earthquake-prone mountains.

title: Drax — a coal plant's reinvention
what: The UK's Drax, once the country's largest coal power station, converted most of its units to burn wood pellets (biomass).
why: It shows one path for retiring coal, though burning imported wood for power remains hotly debated.
care: Its story captures the messy, contested reality of moving a giant fossil plant toward lower carbon.

title: A country's first reactor changes its grid
what: Nations such as Turkey, the UAE, and Bangladesh have recently built their very first nuclear power plants.
why: A first reactor adds a large block of steady, carbon-free power and reshapes a developing nation's energy mix.
care: It marks how nuclear power is spreading to new countries even as some older nuclear nations pull back.

title: Power lines that cross half a country
what: Some transmission links now span more than 3,000 kilometres, carrying power from remote renewable zones to distant cities.
why: Where the sun and wind are best is often far from where people live; these lines bridge that gap.
care: Building long-distance transmission is becoming as important to clean energy as building the wind and solar farms themselves.

title: The Pacific DC Intertie
what: A high-voltage DC line runs about 1,360 kilometres from the Columbia River in Oregon to Los Angeles, one of North America's first major HVDC links.
why: It ships surplus Northwest hydropower south to California in summer and lets the regions back each other up.
care: Operating since 1970, it proved long-distance HVDC works — the same idea now central to moving renewables across continents.
src: U.S. Dept. of Energy / Bonneville Power Administration
